_meta.authoringNotes: Clinical evidence from IDSA SSTI guidelines Stevens et al. 2014 (PMID 24947530). Pathophysiology and microbiology from Swartz MN 2004 (NEJM PMID 15175435). Sepsis pathways referenced to Herold 2023 review.

h1Title: Acute Lymphangitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute lymphangitis is a rapid, potentially serious infection of the lymphatic vessels, characteristically presenting as bright red streaks spreading from a wound, abscess, or skin infection toward regional lymph nodes. Group A Streptococcus pyogenes causes the majority of cases; Staphylococcus aureus, including methicillin-resistant strains, accounts for most of the remainder. The red streaks — the most recognizable clinical sign — follow the anatomical course of superficial lymphatic channels and mark an ascending infection that can escalate to bacteremia, sepsis, and widespread soft tissue infection within hours if not treated promptly. Oral or intravenous beta-lactam or penicillinase-resistant antibiotics are the cornerstone of treatment, with outcomes excellent when therapy begins early.

keyStats.prevalence: No population-based incidence data exists specifically for lymphangitis; it is estimated to complicate 5-15% of significant skin and soft tissue infections managed in urgent care and emergency settings
keyStats.demographics: All ages susceptible; adults 20-50 with traumatic skin wounds, insect bites, and skin infections are the most commonly affected group; immunocompromised individuals have heightened severity
keyStats.avgAge: No dominant age group; presentation clusters with skin trauma and outdoor activity; seen across all ages in clinical practice
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated to affect millions annually worldwide given the enormous burden of skin infections; no WHO or CDC global count available

definition:
Acute lymphangitis (ICD-10: L03.91) is an acute infection and inflammation of the lymphatic vessels caused most commonly by group A Streptococcus pyogenes or Staphylococcus aureus spreading from a primary focus of skin or soft tissue infection. The term encompasses both superficial lymphangitis — the classic red-streak presentation ascending along subcutaneous lymphatic channels — and the less common deep lymphangitis affecting muscular compartments and deeper fascial planes.

The mechanism begins with bacterial invasion at a skin entry point — a wound, abrasion, insect bite, skin ulcer, fungal skin infection such as tinea pedis, or intravenous drug injection site. Bacteria are carried by lymphatic flow from the infected dermis to the regional lymph nodes. The lymphatic walls become infiltrated with neutrophils and macrophages, producing the pathognomonic red streak. Perilymphatic edema, tender lymph node enlargement at the terminus of the streak, and systemic signs of infection follow in proportion to the virulence of the organism and the immune status of the host.

Untreated, lymphangitis can progress to bacteremia (estimated 5-15% of severe cases), sepsis, necrotizing fasciitis, or osteomyelitis. The ICD-10 code L03.91 covers the unspecified-site form; site-specific codes exist for lymphangitis of each limb region, trunk, face, and head. Management falls primarily under dermatology, emergency medicine, and infectious disease.

typesClassification.description: The classic form — one or more bright red, tender linear streaks originating at a distal wound and tracking toward regional lymph nodes. Usually caused by group A Streptococcus; responds well to penicillin or amoxicillin. Associated fever and regional lymphadenitis are common.

Less likely to produce the classic streak; more often presents as induration and tenderness along the lymphatic course, sometimes with nodular swellings. CA-MRSA increasingly recognized; requires empirical MRSA-active therapy in endemic areas.

Caused by Sporothrix schenckii (sporotrichosis) — producing a nodular lymphangitis pattern with sequential nodular lesions ascending the limb — and by filarial worms (Wuchereria bancrofti) in tropical regions. Both require distinct treatments unrelated to antibacterials.

Infection within deeper lymphatic channels beneath the deep fascia; presents with tenderness and induration in the limb without visible surface streak; may underlie necrotizing fasciitis. CT scan required to assess depth and detect gas formation.

primarySymptoms: One or more bright red linear streaks arising from a primary infection focus — wound, puncture, blister, or abscess — and extending proximally toward the groin, axilla, or neck along the course of superficial lymphatic channels.
Tenderness and pain along the streak and in the draining lymph node group at the terminus, typically the inguinal, axillary, or cervical nodes depending on the affected limb or body region.
Fever above 38°C (100.4°F), chills, and systemic malaise, often appearing within hours of the streak's onset and preceding the patient's visit to care.
Warmth and erythema at the site of the primary infection that has spread to involve a linear zone extending several centimeters proximally beyond the initial wound margins.
Tender, enlarged lymph node at the end of the streak indicating lymphadenitis secondary to the ascending infection.
Edema of the affected limb distal and proximal to the streak, resulting from impaired lymphatic drainage and perilymphatic inflammation.
Rapid progression of erythema and streak extension over 12-24 hours in untreated cases, sometimes advancing several centimeters per hour in severe streptococcal infections.
Tachycardia, diaphoresis, and altered mental status in cases complicated by bacteremia or early sepsis, representing a medical emergency requiring immediate intravenous antibiotic therapy.
earlyWarningSigns: A faint, narrow red line emerging from an infected wound, insect bite, or skin ulcer within 12-24 hours of primary infection onset — often the first visible sign of lymphatic spread.
Progressive proximal extension of the red streak over 4-6 hours despite initial wound care, indicating inadequate local containment and the need for systemic antibiotics.
Fever onset accompanying a known skin infection, even before the streak becomes clearly visible, particularly in wounds on the hands or feet from which lymphangitis commonly arises.
New tenderness in the groin, axilla, or neck in a patient known to have an active distal skin infection — suggesting subclinical lymphangitis tracking toward regional nodes.
emergencySigns: Fever above 39°C with a rapidly extending streak and tachycardia (heart rate over 100 bpm) — signs of systemic inflammatory response syndrome requiring intravenous antibiotics and close monitoring for bacteremia.

Streak involving the proximal thigh or groin with swelling of the lower leg and hemodynamic instability — risk of sepsis originating from inguinal lymph-node breakdown or deeper fascial space infection.
Purple or violaceous discoloration replacing the erythematous streak, skin blistering, or crepitus on palpation — signs of necrotizing fasciitis requiring emergency surgical consultation.
Confusion, hypotension, or oliguria in a patient with a known active infection and red streaks — indicators of septic shock requiring immediate resuscitation and ICU evaluation.

causes.description: The dominant cause of acute lymphangitis, responsible for the classic ascending red-streak presentation. Streptococcal toxins (streptolysin O and S, hyaluronidase, and DNAse) facilitate rapid tissue spread along lymphatic channels. Penicillin and amoxicillin are highly effective; resistance is essentially absent.

Second most common cause; produces a less classic streak and more frequently associated with wound abscesses. Community-acquired MRSA strains with panton-valentine leukocidin genes are increasingly recognized and require non-beta-lactam antibiotics. Incision and drainage of any associated abscess is mandatory regardless of antibiotic choice.

causes.cause: Tinea pedis (athlete's foot) or other skin-barrier disruption
causes.description: Interdigital tinea pedis creates fissures and macerated skin that serve as bacterial entry ports for streptococcal or staphylococcal lymphangitis originating on the foot or lower leg. Treating underlying fungal infection with topical antifungals reduces recurrent lower-limb lymphangitis by removing the entry-point reservoir.

Non-sterile injection technique introduces skin flora directly into subcutaneous tissues, producing wound infection and secondary lymphangitis tracking to inguinal or axillary nodes. Polymicrobial infections including anaerobes are more common in this context, often requiring broader-spectrum antibiotic coverage.

Cat bites introduce Pasteurella multocida and Capnocytophaga canimorsus into deep tissues; dog bites carry a polymicrobial load. Both can produce ascending lymphangitis within 12-24 hours; amoxicillin-clavulanate is the antibiotic of choice for bite wounds with spreading infection.

A dimorphic fungus introduced by plant thorns, splinters, or rose thorns that produces a nodular lymphangitis pattern with sequential subcutaneous nodules ascending along the limb over weeks. Classically associated with rose gardening. Treatment requires itraconazole for 3-6 months, not antibacterials.

riskFactors.factor: Active skin infection or open wound
riskFactors.description: Any break in skin integrity — laceration, puncture, insect bite, eczema, fungal infection, or venous ulcer — that allows bacterial entry is the most direct antecedent risk factor. Wound care and early antibiotic treatment of primary infections prevent lymphangitis.

Pre-existing lymphedema significantly impairs lymphatic flow and local immune surveillance, making recurrent lymphangitis up to 15-20 times more likely. Each episode of lymphangitis worsens underlying lymphedema in a self-perpetuating cycle.

Diabetes mellitus, HIV infection, corticosteroids, and chemotherapy weaken neutrophil and lymphocyte responses, promoting more rapid ascending infection and higher rates of bacteremia. Diabetic foot infections with ascending lymphangitis carry a 15-25% amputation risk in poorly controlled patients.

Non-sterile injection introduces skin bacteria directly into subcutaneous tissue; inguinal and axillary lymphangitis and lymphadenitis are among the most common complications, along with endocarditis and deep abscess.

Adipose tissue has poor lymphatic vascularity and provides a nutrient-rich substrate for bacterial growth; obesity is an independent risk factor for lower-limb cellulitis and lymphangitis (OR 2.2, Björnsdóttir et al. 2005).

riskFactors.factor: Tinea pedis and chronic foot skin conditions
riskFactors.description: Interdigital fungal infections on the foot are present in 50-65% of patients presenting with lower-limb lymphangitis; eradicating tinea with topical antifungal therapy reduces the entry-port and decreases recurrence substantially.

affectedDemographics: Adults 20-50 with outdoor or manual occupations exposing hands and feet to soil, vegetation, and trauma account for the largest share of acute presentations.
Lower-limb involvement is most common, reflecting the high frequency of foot wounds, tinea pedis, and venous leg ulcers as entry points.
Intravenous drug users represent a disproportionate share of axillary and inguinal lymphangitis in inner-city emergency departments.
Patients with chronic lymphedema — including cancer survivors after lymph-node dissection, particularly post-breast cancer treatment — face recurrent episodes throughout their lifetime.
diagnosisOverview:
Acute lymphangitis is primarily a clinical diagnosis, made on the basis of the characteristic linear red streak originating from a proximal skin infection or wound and extending toward regional lymph nodes, in the context of fever and systemic illness. The IDSA skin and soft tissue infection guidelines (2014) define lymphangitis as a clinical finding requiring no confirmatory test before starting empirical antibiotics in straightforward presentations.

Blood tests — full blood count, CRP, lactate, and blood cultures — are drawn in febrile patients to quantify systemic inflammation, detect bacteremia, and guide antibiotic de-escalation once cultures return. Leukocytosis with neutrophilia is expected; lactate above 2 mmol/L indicates tissue hypoperfusion and mandates urgent IV therapy.

Imaging is reserved for cases where the diagnosis is uncertain, the patient is not improving on appropriate antibiotics, or necrotizing fasciitis is suspected: ultrasound detects deep abscess collections and fascial thickening; CT scan with contrast identifies fascial-plane gas (pathognomonic of necrotizing fasciitis), deep abscess extent, and adjacent bone involvement. MRI is the most sensitive modality for early deep soft-tissue infection but is used primarily when CT is non-diagnostic.

Wound swab or aspirate culture is obtained whenever pus is present, to guide narrow-spectrum therapy and identify CA-MRSA. Cultures from the lymph node are obtained by fine-needle aspiration in cases with significant lymphadenitis that do not respond to empirical treatment. In sporotrichosis-pattern nodular lymphangitis, wound biopsy and fungal culture are required.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical assessment with wound inspection and marking of streak extent
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms lymphangitis diagnosis and provides a baseline for monitoring progression or regression after antibiotic initiation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician examines the wound and traces the streak with a skin-marker pen or biro, recording the leading edge and time. Re-examination in 4-12 hours determines whether the streak is advancing or regressing. No equipment beyond a pen needed; performed at bedside.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, CRP, and serum lactate
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies systemic inflammation, distinguishes mild localized infection from early sepsis, and determines need for IV versus oral antibiotic management
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine arm vein blood draw. Results in 1-2 hours. Lactate is the most urgent result — above 2 mmol/L with tachycardia triggers sepsis management protocol.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood cultures (aerobic and anaerobic, two sets)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bacteremia and provides organism-specific antibiotic susceptibility data for de-escalating or broadening therapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is drawn from two separate sites into culture bottles. Incubated for 5 days; preliminary positive results flag within 12-24 hours of collection. Negative cultures at 5 days allow confident step-down from broad-spectrum coverage.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism from the primary wound, especially important for detecting CA-MRSA, Pasteurella (bite wounds), or polymicrobial infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile swab is rolled across the wound base or a small-gauge needle is passed into any pus collection. Minimal discomfort. Gram stain result in 24 hours; final culture and sensitivity in 2-5 days.

diagnosticTests.test: CT scan of the affected limb or region with intravenous contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates depth of infection, identifies deep abscess, gas in fascial planes (necrotizing fasciitis), or bone involvement when clinical examination is limited or deterioration continues on antibiotics
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 5-10 minutes in a CT scanner with contrast dye injected into a vein. A mild warm flush during injection is normal. Results reviewed by radiologist within 1-2 hours in emergency settings.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment must begin promptly because streptococcal lymphangitis can spread several centimeters per hour in untreated or inadequately treated cases. IDSA guidelines (Stevens et al. 2014) stratify management by severity and organism probability.

For mild-to-moderate lymphangitis without systemic compromise, oral antibiotics for 5-10 days are appropriate: amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily, or cefalexin 500 mg four times daily, target MSSA and streptococci. When CA-MRSA prevalence locally exceeds 20% or when previous MRSA infection or colonization is documented, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS twice daily or clindamycin 300-450 mg three times daily should be added or substituted.

For moderate-to-severe lymphangitis — marked by fever above 38.5°C, rapidly advancing streak, or lymphadenitis with signs of abscess — intravenous antibiotics are indicated: IV penicillin G 2-4 million units every 4-6 hours for suspected pure streptococcal infection; IV nafcillin or oxacillin 1-2 g every 4 hours for MSSA; IV vancomycin 25-30 mg/kg/day in divided doses for MRSA; IV ampicillin-sulbactam or piperacillin-tazobactam for polymicrobial or bite-wound infections.

For bite-wound-associated lymphangitis, amoxicillin-clavulanate is the first-line oral agent, covering Pasteurella, Capnocytophaga, streptococci, and anaerobes. Dog bite-associated infections should be assessed for rabies prophylaxis need.

Sporothrix lymphangitis requires oral itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 3-6 months; terbinafine is an alternative in patients who cannot tolerate itraconazole. Supersaturated potassium iodide was the historical treatment but is now largely replaced.

Tinea pedis in the entry-zone foot must be treated concurrently with topical clotrimazole or miconazole to prevent recurrence. Limb elevation, analgesics, and warm compresses are adjuvant measures for all forms.

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (875/125 mg orally twice daily, 5-10 days)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral treatment for MSSA-streptococcal lymphangitis and bite-wound-associated lymphangitis. Beta-lactamase inhibitor clavulanate extends coverage to Pasteurella and anaerobes. Take with food to reduce gastrointestinal upset.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of lymphangitis symptoms in 85-95% of non-MRSA cases in observational series; standard of care per IDSA 2014 guidelines

medicalTreatments.name: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS (one tablet twice daily, 5-10 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred when CA-MRSA is suspected (purulent presentation, prior MRSA, household MRSA contact). Active against most CA-MRSA strains in the US. Avoid in sulfa allergy, late pregnancy, or severe renal impairment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent to clindamycin for CA-MRSA outpatient skin infections in RCTs (Miller et al. 2015); no RCT specific to lymphangitis

medicalTreatments.name: IV penicillin G (3-4 million units every 4-6 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred intravenous therapy for documented pure streptococcal lymphangitis. Group A streptococcus has no acquired penicillin resistance. Step down to oral amoxicillin after clinical improvement (typically 48-72 hours of IV therapy).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Near 100% microbiological cure rate for group A strep infections; clinical improvement typically within 24-48 hours

medicalTreatments.name: Vancomycin IV (25-30 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line IV therapy for MRSA lymphangitis, MRSA bacteremia from lymphangitis, and patients with penicillin anaphylaxis. AUC/MIC-guided dosing. Continue until hemodynamically stable and able to step down to oral doxycycline or TMP-SMX.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 80-90% clinical success for MRSA skin and soft tissue infections in meta-analyses; bacteremia requires 2-6 weeks total course

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole (200 mg twice daily for 3-6 months) for sporotrichosis
medicalTreatments.description: Oral azole antifungal targeting Sporothrix schenckii in lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis. Must be taken with a meal for adequate absorption. Response confirmed by resolution of subcutaneous nodules.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates 90-100% in lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis in prospective series; relapse under 5% when full course completed

medicalTreatments.name: Clindamycin (300-450 mg orally three times daily, 5-10 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Covers CA-MRSA, MSSA, and streptococci; active against anaerobes relevant to bite or polymicrobial wound infections. D-zone test recommended to exclude inducible macrolide-lincosamide-streptogramin resistance in CA-MRSA isolates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to TMP-SMX for outpatient CA-MRSA SSTI in equivalence RCTs; useful when renal impairment precludes TMP-SMX

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage of abscess at primary infection site
surgicalOptions.description: Mandatory when the primary focus of lymphangitis includes a fluctuant abscess. Draining the source reduces bacterial inoculum and accelerates clinical response. Adjuvant antibiotics prevent post-drainage bacteremia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution in 85-95% of superficial abscess-associated lymphangitis when drainage is combined with antibiotics; recurrence 5-15% without antibiotics per Loop RCT 2017

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debridement for necrotizing fasciitis
surgicalOptions.description: Emergency surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue is required when necrotizing fasciitis complicates deep lymphangitis; delay beyond 12-24 hours increases mortality significantly. Wide excision with reconstruction follows after infection control.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mortality 20-35% even with prompt surgery; delay over 24 hours from diagnosis associated with 2-3 times higher mortality in retrospective cohort studies

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical antifungals (clotrimazole, terbinafine cream) for coexisting tinea pedis
alternativeTreatments.description: Treating the interdigital fungal entry port with topical antifungals prevents recurrent streptococcal lymphangitis; two randomized trials found recurrence rates halved in patients with tinea pedis who completed antifungal treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: Any patient with an advancing red streak accompanied by fever over 38.5°C, a rapidly progressing wound, systemic toxicity, or a streak that has not stabilized within 12-24 hours of oral antibiotics should be evaluated urgently in an emergency department. Infectious disease or dermatology specialist input is valuable for recurrent lymphangitis, suspected CA-MRSA, bite wounds, nodular lymphangitis patterns suggesting sporotrichosis, or lymphangitis in immunocompromised patients.
doctorSelectionGuide: The initial clinician should be comfortable performing wound assessment and drainage, prescribing MRSA-active empirical antibiotics in appropriate epidemiologic settings, and recognizing early sepsis. Patients with recurrent lower-limb lymphangitis should be evaluated by a dermatologist or venous and lymphatic medicine specialist for underlying lymphedema, tinea pedis, or venous insufficiency contributing to recurrence.
hospitalCriteria: Fever above 38.5°C with rapidly advancing streak and tachycardia — inpatient IV antibiotics and serial clinical assessment required
Signs of early sepsis: lactate above 2 mmol/L, tachycardia, hypotension, or altered consciousness
Failure of 24-48 hours of appropriate oral antibiotics with clinical progression
Immunocompromised patients with any degree of lymphangitis given higher bacteremia risk
keyFacilities: Emergency departments for sepsis evaluation and IV antibiotic initiation
Dermatology clinics for recurrent or atypical presentations
Infectious disease inpatient services for bacteremia or MRSA management
Vascular and lymphedema specialist units for recurrent lower-limb cases
preventionStrategies: Clean all wounds promptly with soap and water or antiseptic, cover with a sterile dressing, and change daily until healed to prevent bacterial colonization at entry points.
Treat tinea pedis and other fungal skin infections promptly with topical antifungals to eliminate the most common entry portal for lower-limb streptococcal lymphangitis.
Seek early medical evaluation for any wound that becomes increasingly red, warm, or tender; early antibiotic treatment of skin infections prevents lymphatic spread.
Manage chronic lymphedema with compression therapy, meticulous skin care, and prompt treatment of even minor skin breaks, since lymphedema dramatically amplifies lymphangitis risk.
Wear appropriate gloves and protective footwear during gardening, soil work, and occupational activities involving contact with potentially infected materials.
lifestyleModifications: Elevate the affected limb above heart level when resting to reduce lymphatic and venous congestion and speed resolution of the streak and edema.
Complete the full antibiotic course; discontinuing early when symptoms improve — a common patient behavior — risks incomplete eradication and recurrence.
Mark the streak border with a skin marker at the time of diagnosis to objectively track progression or regression; progression requires urgent medical reassessment.
Apply warm moist compresses to the infected area for pain relief; avoid ice, which constricts local blood flow and may impair antibiotic delivery to the infection site.
Seek emergency care immediately if the streak advances despite 12-24 hours of oral antibiotics, if new fever spikes occur, or if confusion or hypotension develops.

recommended: High fluid intake (at least 2 L/day) to support renal clearance of antibiotic metabolites and maintain blood pressure during febrile illness
Protein-rich foods (lean meat, eggs, beans) to provide substrate for immune cell proliferation and tissue repair
Vitamin C-rich foods to support collagen synthesis in healing wound tissue
Probiotic-containing foods (yogurt, kefir) during antibiotic treatment to mitigate antibiotic-associated diarrhea, particularly with clindamycin
avoid: Alcohol during antibiotic therapy, especially with metronidazole (causes disulfiram reaction) and clindamycin (increases Clostridioides difficile risk)
High-sugar, high-refined-carbohydrate foods in diabetic patients, as hyperglycemia directly impairs neutrophil function and slows wound healing

exerciseGuidelines: Complete rest of the affected limb is recommended during the acute phase of lymphangitis. Walking should be minimal when the streak involves the leg. Once fever resolves and the streak has stabilized or regressed, gentle range-of-motion exercises and short walks can resume. Return to full activity, including work, is appropriate once the streak has fully resolved and the wound is healing — typically 7-14 days after starting treatment.
dailyManagement: Take antibiotics at evenly spaced intervals to maintain therapeutic blood levels; set phone reminders to avoid missed doses.
Inspect the marked streak border twice daily and re-consult promptly if the streak advances beyond the marked boundary despite 12-24 hours of antibiotic treatment.
Keep the wound clean, dry between compress applications, and covered with a fresh sterile dressing each day.
Monitor temperature twice daily and record findings; share with the treating clinician at follow-up.
Elevate the limb when sitting or lying down to minimize lymphedema and hasten resolution of the streak and associated swelling.
prognosis: Acute lymphangitis carries an excellent prognosis when treated early and appropriately. The majority of streptococcal and staphylococcal cases respond within 24-48 hours of starting appropriate antibiotics, with the streak regressing proximally and fever resolving. Full clinical resolution occurs in 5-10 days for mild cases. Bacteremia complicates approximately 5-15% of severe cases not receiving timely treatment, and this significantly prolongs hospitalization and increases mortality risk from septic emboli. Recurrent lymphangitis, particularly in patients with underlying lymphedema or tinea pedis, is common without treatment of the predisposing condition; up to 30-40% of lymphedema patients experience one or more episodes of lymphangitis annually. Sporotrichosis-pattern nodular lymphangitis resolves in over 90% of patients with a full course of itraconazole. The most dangerous outcome — necrotizing fasciitis — occurs in under 1% of lymphangitis cases but carries a mortality rate of 20-35% even with prompt surgical management.
recoveryTimeline: Mild oral-antibiotic-treated cases: fever resolution within 24-48 hours; streak regression within 2-5 days; full wound healing 7-14 days. Hospitalized IV-treated cases: clinical improvement within 48-72 hours; oral step-down after 3-5 days IV; total course 7-14 days. Recurrence with underlying lymphedema: ongoing; managed with long-term prophylactic benzathine penicillin in recurrent streptococcal cases (monthly IM injection reduces annual episode frequency by 60%).
complications: Bacteremia and sepsis — 5-15% of severe or untreated lymphangitis cases develop bloodstream infection; detected by blood cultures and managed with IV antibiotics in hospital.

Necrotizing fasciitis — deep extension of infection into fascial planes, recognized by rapidly advancing borders, skin discoloration or blistering, and crepitus; surgical emergency with mortality 20-35%.
Suppurative lymphadenitis — the regional lymph node terminus of the streak may form an abscess requiring drainage; nodes that fail to resolve after 6 weeks require biopsy.

Chronic lymphedema — recurrent lymphangitis damages lymphatic vessel walls and valves, permanently impairing lymphatic drainage of the affected limb; progressive limb swelling follows repeated episodes.

Osteomyelitis — rare complication of lymphangitis from contiguous spread of a deeply seated wound infection, particularly in diabetic foot lesions; suspected when bone pain or drainage persists despite adequate antibiotic therapy.
Antibiotic-associated Clostridioides difficile colitis — particularly with clindamycin use; present with new diarrhea more than 3 days after antibiotic initiation.

supportResources.name: IDSA Skin and Soft Tissue Infection Guidelines
supportResources.description: IDSA clinical practice guidelines for practitioners covering diagnosis and antimicrobial treatment of lymphangitis, cellulitis, and related skin infections.

supportResources.name: CDC — MRSA Information for Patients
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on community-acquired MRSA skin infections, wound care, and antibiotic stewardship for patients and families.

confusedWithConditions.name: Erysipelas
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Erysipelas is a sharply demarcated, elevated, bright-red skin plate involving the dermis and superficial lymphatics — a broad confluent area, not a linear streak. It advances as an expanding plaque rather than a linear streak. Both are caused by streptococci and treated with penicillin, but erysipelas lacks the characteristic streak-plus-distal-wound pattern of lymphangitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute lymphadenitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute lymphadenitis is the inflammation of a lymph node itself, presenting as a focal tender swelling in the groin, axilla, or neck without the upstream red streak. Lymphangitis produces the streak in the vessels leading to the node; lymphadenitis is the inflammation of the node at the streak's destination. Both frequently coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atypical mycobacterial infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Non-tuberculous mycobacterial lymphangitis progresses slowly over weeks to months rather than hours, produces nodular lesions rather than a streak, and does not respond to antibacterial antibiotics. Sporotrichosis mimics this pattern even more closely; both require fungal or mycobacterial culture from aspirated nodules for definitive diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Acute lymphadenitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Lymphadenitis of the regional node at the terminus of the lymphangitic streak is present in the majority of cases; the two conditions are part of the same ascending infection process.

relatedConditions.name: Erysipelas
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are superficial streptococcal skin and lymphatic infections treated with penicillin; erysipelas affects broad skin plates while lymphangitis tracks along individual lymphatic channels.

relatedConditions.name: Atypical mycobacterial infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Nodular lymphangitis caused by atypical mycobacteria mimics sporotrichosis and must be differentiated from bacterial lymphangitis to guide correct treatment.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: HIV-related immunosuppression increases the severity and atypical presentation of lymphangitis and lymphadenitis, and opportunistic organisms are more likely to be responsible.

faqs.question: What is acute lymphangitis?
faqs.answer: Acute lymphangitis is an infection of the lymphatic vessels, presenting as a red streak running from a wound or skin infection toward a regional lymph node. It is most often caused by Streptococcus pyogenes or Staphylococcus aureus and requires prompt antibiotic treatment.

faqs.question: What causes the red streak in lymphangitis?
faqs.answer: Bacteria spread from a skin wound through lymphatic vessels toward regional lymph nodes. The vessel walls become inflamed, producing visible red linear streaks that track from the infection site toward the groin, axilla, or neck depending on the location of the wound.

faqs.question: Is lymphangitis an emergency?
faqs.answer: Lymphangitis with advancing red streaks and high fever is a medical urgency requiring same-day evaluation and antibiotic treatment. If the streak advances despite oral antibiotics, or if signs of sepsis appear — confusion, rapid heart rate, low blood pressure — emergency department care is needed immediately.

faqs.question: How is lymphangitis treated?
faqs.answer: Oral antibiotics targeting Streptococcus and Staphylococcus — typically amoxicillin-clavulanate or cefalexin for 5-10 days — are first-line for mild cases. CA-MRSA is treated with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or clindamycin. Intravenous antibiotics are used when fever is high or the streak is advancing rapidly.

faqs.question: How long does lymphangitis last?
faqs.answer: With appropriate antibiotics, fever usually resolves within 24-48 hours and the red streak begins to regress within 2-5 days. Full resolution takes 7-14 days for mild cases. IV-treated cases in hospital typically improve within 48-72 hours before stepping down to oral antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can lymphangitis cause sepsis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated or inadequately treated lymphangitis can progress to bacteremia and sepsis in approximately 5-15% of severe cases. Signs of sepsis — confusion, rapid pulse, low blood pressure, high fever — require immediate emergency evaluation and intravenous antibiotics.

faqs.question: Is lymphangitis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Lymphangitis is not contagious from person to person. It arises from bacteria already present on the patient's own skin or introduced by a wound. You cannot catch lymphangitis by being near or touching a person with the condition.

faqs.question: How do I know if my red streak is lymphangitis?
faqs.answer: A thin, tender, red line running from a wound, bite, or skin infection toward your groin, armpit, or neck — often accompanied by fever — is the hallmark of lymphangitis. Seek medical evaluation the same day; early treatment prevents serious complications.

faqs.question: Can lymphangitis come back?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrent lymphangitis is common in people with chronic lymphedema, tinea pedis, or venous insufficiency. Treating the entry portal — tinea pedis with antifungals, venous ulcers with compression — reduces recurrence. Patients with frequent streptococcal recurrences may benefit from monthly preventive penicillin injections.

faqs.question: What is the difference between lymphangitis and cellulitis?
faqs.answer: Cellulitis is a diffuse, spreading skin and subcutaneous tissue infection producing a broad, poorly defined red area. Lymphangitis produces a distinct linear red streak following lymphatic channels rather than spreading diffusely. Both require antibiotics; distinguishing them guides monitoring and helps identify more serious deep infections.

faqs.question: Can lymphangitis affect the arm?
faqs.answer: Yes. Arm lymphangitis typically arises from hand or wrist wounds, animal bites, or intravenous injection sites and produces a red streak running up the arm toward the axillary lymph nodes. It is treated the same as lower-limb lymphangitis with appropriate antibiotics.

faqs.question: What does nodular lymphangitis mean?
faqs.answer: Nodular lymphangitis is a pattern where sequential subcutaneous nodules form along the lymphatic channel instead of a streak. It is characteristic of sporotrichosis (a fungal infection from plant thorn injuries) or atypical mycobacterial infection. It requires fungal culture and antifungal therapy — not standard antibacterials.

faqs.question: Should I go to the ER for a red streak?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care for a red streak if it is advancing rapidly, accompanied by high fever (above 39°C), rigors, confusion, or low blood pressure. For a stable streak with mild fever, same-day urgent care or a GP visit is appropriate to start antibiotics and monitor the streak border.

faqs.question: Does lymphangitis need hospitalization?
faqs.answer: Mild lymphangitis with stable streaks and low-grade fever can be managed at home with oral antibiotics and close monitoring. Hospitalization is needed for rapidly advancing infection, high fever, signs of sepsis, failure of 24-48 hours of oral antibiotics, or immunocompromising conditions.

faqs.question: Can athlete's foot cause lymphangitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Tinea pedis (athlete's foot) creates fissures in the skin between the toes that allow Streptococcus and Staphylococcus to enter, causing lower-leg lymphangitis. Treating tinea pedis with antifungal cream is an essential step in preventing recurrent lymphangitis in affected patients.

metaTitle: Acute Lymphangitis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Acute lymphangitis: red streaks from infected wounds that can progress to sepsis. Causes, antibiotic treatment, emergency signs, and prevention reviewed here.
